interviewStages: Behavioral: customer focus, ownership, working with B2C scale
codingTopics: Time-series and ML: price prediction over historical fares
Search and graph: flight search across airlines, sub-second latency
Caching: fare cache, memoized search results
Numbers and finance: pricing freeze financial products
systemDesignTopics: Design a flight-price prediction model and serving infrastructure
Design flight search across hundreds of airlines with sub-second latency
Design a freeze-the-price financial product on top of travel booking
Design a price-watch and notification system for millions of users

coding: Given historical fare time-series for a route, predict whether to buy now or wait. Discuss features and the rolling-window computation.
Search flights across airlines modeled as a graph of flight segments to find the cheapest itineraries under a stopover limit. Discuss the approach.
Cache fare-search results with TTL and invalidation on price changes. Discuss the data structure and hit-rate tradeoffs.
Process a stream of price-watch subscriptions and fire notifications when a tracked fare drops below a threshold. Discuss efficiency at scale.
behavioral: Hopper is B2C at huge scale. Tell me about a time you owned a consumer-facing reliability problem.
Describe a time an ML or pricing model was wrong and how you handled the customer impact.
Give an example of shipping a mobile feature and handling real-world edge cases.
Tell me about a time you balanced business margin against customer trust.
systemDesign: Design a flight-price prediction model plus the infrastructure that serves it.
Design flight search across hundreds of airlines with sub-second latency.
Design the freeze-the-price feature, a financial product layered on travel booking.
values: Hopper is deeply data-driven. Describe a time data overturned your intuition about what to build.
Freeze-the-price takes on real financial risk for customers. How do you build trust in such a feature?
Tell me about a time you prioritized long-term customer trust over a short-term win.
other: How does Hopper compare to Booking.com or Expedia?

Updated May 5, 2026 · techinterview.org

⏱ 1 min read

Hopper
hopper.com ↗

Hopper is the mobile-first travel platform that pioneered ML-powered price prediction for flights and hotels. Used by tens of millions for booking and price-watch features. The interview emphasizes mobile engineering, ML for pricing, and the unique constraints of the travel domain.

Process

Recruiter screen → 60-minute coding phone (DSA medium) → onsite virtual: 2 coding, 1 system design, 1 craft deep-dive, 1 behavioral. Cycle: 3–4 weeks.

What they actually ask

- Design a price prediction model and serving infrastructure for flight prices

- Design search across hundreds of airlines with sub-second latency

- Design "freeze the price" features (financial product on top of travel)

- Coding: medium DSA, often with caching or graph framing

- Behavioral: customer focus, ownership, working with B2C scale

Levels and comp (2026)

- SE II: $150K–$190K total (US); CAD$135K–$170K (Montreal)

- Senior SE: $215K–$285K

- Staff: $310K–$410K

- Principal: $430K–$570K

Prep priorities

- Be fluent in Scala (backend) or Python (ML); Swift/Kotlin for mobile roles

- Understand ML system design and time-series forecasting

- Brush up on airline data model: GDS, ATPCO, fare construction

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Hopper remote-friendly?

Hybrid in Montreal (HQ), Boston, Toronto, NYC, London. Some engineering roles fully remote.

How does Hopper compare to Booking.com or Expedia?

Hopper is mobile-first and ML-driven; legacy OTAs are desktop-heavy and inventory-broker-driven. Hopper comp is competitive with FAANG-Canada.

What is the engineering culture?

Strong technical bar, ML-curious, mobile-first. Montreal HQ gives it a different cultural feel from Bay Area peers.
